articles.title: Ice Age Dice Show Native Americans Played Games Long Ago
articles.summary: A long time ago, during the Ice Age, Native Americans made dice to play games of chance. These dice were simple with only two sides, not six like today's dice. They were used for fun and to make decisions in games. A researcher named Robert Madden studied old dice found by archaeologists. He found that Native Americans have been using dice for over 12,000 years, which is much earlier than people in other parts of the world. Madden looked at many dice from different tribes and places. He made rules to tell if an old object is a dice. The dice had to be two-sided, easy to tell apart, a certain shape, and small enough to hold. This discovery shows that Native Americans understood how to use random outcomes in games long before others.

articles.title: How People Stop Thinking When Using AI
articles.summary: Scientists studied how people use AI like chatbots. They found that many people trust AI too much and stop thinking for themselves, which is called "cognitive surrender." In experiments, people were given tricky questions. Some could ask an AI for help, but the AI sometimes gave wrong answers on purpose. Even when the AI was wrong, many people still believed it. This shows that AI can make people lazy thinkers. When people were paid or had more time, they checked the AI's answers better. But when they were rushed, they trusted the AI more. This research warns us to be careful with AI and always think critically.

articles.title: A Tiny Claw in a Very Old Fossil Changes Spider History
articles.summary: Scientists found a tiny claw in a 500-million-year-old fossil, and it tells us something amazing about spiders. The fossil is from a sea creature that lived long ago in Utah. At first, it looked like other old fossils, but when a scientist named Rudy cleaned it, he saw a claw where an antenna should be. This claw is called a chelicera, and it's special because it's the oldest one ever found. Chelicerates are a group of animals that includes spiders, scorpions, and horseshoe crabs. This discovery shows that spiders and their relatives started to look like they do today much earlier than we thought, about 20 million years earlier. The fossil helps us understand how these animals evolved from ancient sea creatures. It's like finding a missing piece in a puzzle about life on Earth.

articles.title: A Special Diet Helps People with Crohn's Disease Feel Better
articles.summary: Scientists studied a special diet to help people with Crohn's disease, a sickness that affects the stomach and intestines. In a big test, they had 97 patients try a diet that mimics fasting for five days each month, where they ate fewer calories and plant-based foods. The rest of the month, they ate normally. After three months, about two-thirds of those on the diet felt better, with less pain and other symptoms, while fewer than half in the control group improved. This diet could help doctors give better advice on what to eat to manage Crohn's disease, which is important because many patients ask about food but there hasn't been much research on it. The study was published in a science journal and involved researchers from Stanford Medicine.

articles.title: Bees Make Homes in Old Animal Bones
articles.summary: Long ago on an island called Hispaniola, a big owl caught a rodent called a hutia and took it to a cave. The owl ate the hutia, leaving its bones on the cave floor. Later, a bee came looking for a place to build its nest. It found the hutia's jawbone, which had small holes where teeth used to be. The bee used these holes as a perfect home. More bees came and did the same thing. Scientists found these bones with bee nests inside them in a cave in the Dominican Republic. They were careful when digging up the bones and noticed the smooth holes. At first, they thought wasps made the nests, but they learned it was bees. This discovery shows how bees can use old bones to live in.

articles.title: How a Gene Change Affects Thinking in Schizophrenia
articles.summary: Schizophrenia is a brain condition where people can have trouble understanding the world around them. Scientists at MIT found a gene change that might cause this problem. This gene is called grin2a. In mice with this change, their brains could not update their thoughts when they learned new things. This makes it hard for them to make good decisions. The researchers think fixing this brain circuit could help people with schizophrenia think better. They studied many genes and found that grin2a is important. This discovery helps us learn more about how the brain works in this condition.
